classes.label: Country
classes.description: Sovereign country identified by database code.

classes.label: Province
classes.description: First-level administrative division within a country.

classes.label: City
classes.description: City identified by name within country and province.

classes.label: Country Population Record
classes.description: Time-indexed population observation for a country.

classes.label: Province Population Record
classes.description: Time-indexed population observation for a province.

classes.label: City Population Record
classes.description: Time-indexed population observation for a city.

classes.label: Spoken Language Usage
classes.description: Reified relation capturing percentage of a language spoken in a country.

classes.label: Ethnic Group Presence
classes.description: Reified relation capturing percentage of an ethnic group in a country.

classes.label: Religion Presence
classes.description: Reified relation capturing percentage of a religion in a country.

assumptions: Foreign keys were not explicitly provided; joins are inferred from naming, primary keys, and common Mondial schema conventions.
One-to-one country aspect tables Economy, Politics, and Population were flattened into data properties of Country rather than modeled as separate classes.
Tables Spoken, EthnicGroup, and Religion carry meaningful percentage attributes, so they were modeled as reified classes rather than simple binary object properties.
No inverse object properties were added.
Columns such as Politics.Dependent and Island.Islands or Mountain.Mountains were not mapped where target semantics were ambiguous without stronger evidence.
coverage_warnings: Some nullable reference columns may not always resolve to target rows.
countryBordersCountry is likely symmetric in meaning, but only one directed property was modeled conservatively.
geo_Estuary was not separately modeled because estuary geography may overlap with River estuary coordinates and target semantics are not fully explicit.
confidence_notes: Core geopolitical entities and their containment relations have high confidence.
Historical population record classes have good structural support from composite primary keys including Year.
A few political and hierarchy-related attributes have lower confidence because names alone do not fully determine intended semantics.
